flashcards.front: What is guided media in computer networks?
flashcards.back: Guided media (also called bounded media) are physical communication channels that transmit data signals along a specific path from sender to receiver. Examples include twisted pair cables, coaxial cables, and fiber optic cables.

flashcards.front: List the three main types of guided media.
flashcards.back: The three main types are: (1) Twisted Pair Cable (UTP/STP), (2) Coaxial Cable, and (3) Fiber Optic Cable (Single-mode and Multi-mode).

flashcards.front: What is the difference between UTP and STP cables?
flashcards.back: UTP (Unshielded Twisted Pair) has no metallic shielding, making it susceptible to EMI. STP (Shielded Twisted Pair) has metallic shielding around each wire pair, providing better protection against EMI and crosstalk.

flashcards.front: What are the EIA/TIA categories for twisted pair cables?
flashcards.back: Categories include: Cat3 (10 Mbps), Cat5 (100 Mbps), Cat5e (1 Gbps), Cat6 (1 Gbps/10 Gbps), Cat6a (10 Gbps), Cat7 (10 Gbps), and Cat8 (25-40 Gbps).

flashcards.front: What is the maximum cable length for twisted pair cables?
flashcards.back: The maximum recommended length for twisted pair cables (10BASE-T/100BASE-TX) is 100 meters (328 feet) before signal degradation occurs.

flashcards.front: What are the two types of coaxial cables?
flashcards.back: The two main types are: (1) Thinnet (10BASE2) - thin coaxial cable, approximately 5mm diameter, max 185m length, and (2) Thicknet (10BASE5) - thick coaxial cable, approximately 10mm diameter, max 500m length.

flashcards.front: What is the core diameter difference between single-mode and multi-mode fiber?
flashcards.back: Single-mode fiber has a core diameter of 8-10 micrometers, while multi-mode fiber has a core diameter of 50-62.5 micrometers, allowing multiple light modes.

flashcards.front: What is the maximum distance for fiber optic cables?
flashcards.back: Single-mode fiber can carry signals up to 100km without repeaters, while multi-mode fiber is limited to approximately 2km for 100Mbps and 550m for 1Gbps.

flashcards.front: What are the advantages of fiber optic cables over copper?
flashcards.back: Advantages include: much higher bandwidth, lower attenuation (longer distances), immunity to EMI, better security (difficult to tap), lighter weight, and no electrical interference.

flashcards.front: What is attenuation in guided media?
flashcards.back: Attenuation is the gradual loss of signal strength as it travels through the medium. It is measured in decibels (dB) and increases with distance and frequency.

flashcards.front: What is crosstalk in twisted pair cables?
flashcards.back: Crosstalk is the interference caused by signals from adjacent wire pairs. Near-end crosstalk (NEXT) occurs at the sending end, while far-end crosstalk (FEXT) occurs at the receiving end.

flashcards.front: What is the role of a repeater in guided media transmission?
flashcards.back: A repeater amplifies and regenerates weakened signals in long cable runs. It extends the maximum network segment length and helps overcome attenuation.

flashcards.front: What are the common connectors used with twisted pair cables?
flashcards.back: Common connectors include RJ-45 (8-pin) for Ethernet connections, and RJ-11 (6-pin) for telephone connections.

flashcards.front: What are the typical connector types for fiber optic cables?
flashcards.back: Common fiber connectors include SC (Subscriber Connector), ST (Straight Tip), LC (Lucent Connector), and MTRJ (Mechanical Transfer Registered Jack).
